AutoGen SelectorGroupChat (autogen_agentchat.teams) with a MODEL-BASED selector, not RoundRobin. Five participants are passed in order [data_expert, code_expert, research_expert, writing_expert, planner]; per the node-id convention each node id is the Python variable name (all happen to equal the agents' name= too).

Routing: on every turn a GPT-4o OpenAIChatCompletionClient runs `selector_prompt` over {roles}/{history}/{participants} and returns one agent name (or 'NONE' to stop). The selector is framework machinery, NOT a participant, so there is no separate selector/router node per the given conventions — instead the model-based dispatch is represented on the edges. Because the selector may pick ANY participant as the first speaker and ANY other participant on each subsequent turn, the concrete per-turn transition sequence is undeterminable from static source. I therefore encoded the full reachability the selector permits: __start__ -> every agent (conditional first-speaker dispatch), every agent -> every other agent (loop re-selection), and every agent -> __end__ (conditional termination). Self-loops are omitted because SelectorGroupChat's allow_repeated_speaker defaults to False (no consecutive same speaker).

The loop is bounded by MaxMessageTermination(8) (ends after 8 messages); the selector prompt also biases the model to pick `planner` first ("Make sure the planner agent has assigned tasks before other agents start working"), so in practice planner is the likely entry agent, but statically any agent is reachable from __start__.

All five agents are plain AssistantAgents sharing one gpt-4o client with only a system_message and description — no tools/functions bound — hence kind=llm for all (none are tool nodes). Console(stream)/team.run_stream(task=...) in main() are execution/UI plumbing, not workflow nodes. Confidence medium: the participant set, framework, model-based-selection semantics, and termination bound are certain from source; the exact edge transitions are runtime/model-decided, so the dense all-to-all edge set represents permitted reachability rather than a guaranteed control-flow path.

This file is an AutoGen Studio component-gallery builder (create_default_gallery), NOT a single executable workflow. It registers 4 models, several standalone tools/terminations, and FOUR independent teams into a catalog via GalleryBuilder. Confidence is low for that reason.

The 4 teams (by participant variable names, per the AutoGen id convention = agent variable name):
- calc_team: RoundRobinGroupChat([calc_assistant]) — single agent; round-robin last->first yields a self-loop on calc_assistant.
- selector_default_team: SelectorGroupChat([calc_assistant, critic_agent]).
- websurfer_team: SelectorGroupChat([websurfer_agent, verification_assistant, web_user_proxy]).
- deep_research_team: SelectorGroupChat([research_assistant, verifier, summary_agent]).

I unioned all four teams into one graph: __start__ fans out to each team's first participant, and each team's TERMINATE-emitting agent connects to __end__ (calc_assistant for teams 1/2 per its system_message; verification_assistant for the websurfer team; summary_agent for the deep-research team). calc_assistant is reused across teams 1 and 2, so it is a single node. Note: calc_assistant and verification_assistant share the AssistantAgent name= "assistant_agent", but the id convention uses the Python variable name, so they are distinct nodes.

Caveat on edges: 3 of the 4 teams are SelectorGroupChat, where a selector LLM routes to any participant in any order (router-mediated, effectively fully connected among participants). Because the id/edge convention only specifies round-robin loop behavior and one-node-per-participant (no separate selector node), I approximated the selector teams as sequential participant chains with a loopback edge (kind=loop) to align with the extractor. The true selector semantics are conditional any-order transitions, so intra-team direct/loop edges are an approximation of order, not literal control flow. web_user_proxy is a human-in-the-loop node (UserProxyAgent), consulted only when verification stalls. Tool bindings: calc_assistant->calculator_tool, research_assistant->google_search_tool+fetch_webpage_tool, websurfer_agent uses an internal headless browser. The GalleryBuilder/GalleryConfig plumbing and __main__ JSON dump are config serialization, not workflow control flow, so they are omitted.

Kinds: InitialWriterAgent and CriticAgent are plain LLM Agents (only output_key, no tools). RefinerAgent has tools=[FunctionTool(exit_loop)] bound, hence kind "tool"; exit_loop is a local Python function returning an approval dict that signals the LoopAgent to stop. google_search / AgentTool are imported but never bound to any agent, so no tool edges from those.

Root is an ADK SequentialAgent ("video_system_orchestrator") that runs 6 imported sub-agents strictly in sequence: research -> story -> asset_sourcing -> image_generation -> audio -> video_assembly. Modeled as __start__ -> chain -> __end__ with all direct edges per ADK SequentialAgent semantics.

Node ids use the variable names as passed into sub_agents=[...] (research_agent, story_agent, asset_sourcing_agent, image_generation_agent, audio_agent, video_assembly_agent). Each sub-agent is imported as `root_agent` from its own module and aliased; the actual .name strings are defined in those other files and are not visible in this source, so the aliased variable names are used as ids.

All 6 sub-agents are classified as 'llm' (agent-reasoning steps) because this file does not reveal whether any have tools bound. Names strongly imply external tool/API use (image_generation, audio, video_assembly especially), so some may truly be 'tool' nodes in their own definitions — undeterminable from this file alone.

The `else` branch (ADK unavailable -> root_agent = None, with a mock SequentialAgent) is an import-guard fallback, not a distinct workflow path, so it is omitted. Graph structure at the orchestrator level is fully static and unambiguous; confidence high.

groundTruth.notes:

No routers, loops, parallel fan-out, or human-in-the-loop steps exist statically in this file.

The file defines a real ADK workflow via nested SequentialAgents. Root `MarketingOrchestratorAgent` (SequentialAgent) has sub_agents = [BusinessAnalysisAgent, ContentGenerationAgent]. BusinessAnalysisAgent (SequentialAgent) = [URLAnalysisAgent, FileAnalysisAgent, BusinessContextAgent]; ContentGenerationAgent (SequentialAgent) = [SocialContentAgent, HashtagOptimizationAgent]. I flattened the two intermediate SequentialAgents into their leaf agents, since SequentialAgent chaining makes the true execution a single linear sequence of the 5 leaf LlmAgents. An alternative valid rendering would keep BusinessAnalysisAgent and ContentGenerationAgent as two `subgraph` nodes chained at the top level; I chose the flattened leaf chain as the more faithful "what actually runs" ground truth.

Node kinds: URLAnalysisAgent, FileAnalysisAgent, BusinessContextAgent are plain LlmAgents with no tools bound (kind=llm). SocialContentAgent and HashtagOptimizationAgent each have `tools=[...]` bound (generate_social_posts / optimize_hashtags), so kind=tool per the "framework binds tools" rule. IDs use the LlmAgent `name=` strings, which match the extractor convention (sub_agent name). Caveat lowering confidence to medium: the runtime entry point `execute_campaign_workflow` does NOT actually invoke these ADK agents — it calls plain helper functions (_extract_business_context_from_description, _generate_real_social_content, _format_generated_content) and a separate VisualContentOrchestratorAgent (imported, not defined here). Source is a UNIT TEST file (test_extract_adk.py) for the ADK extractor, using local stub classes (_StubAgent + SequentialAgent/ParallelAgent/LoopAgent/LlmAgent) that mimic real google-adk agents. It is NOT a single real workflow definition — hence confidence=low. It contains 8 independent test fixtures, each building a tiny agent and calling extract_adk(). I encoded the UNION of all fixtures because every node/edge genuinely exists in the source; they share the single __start__/__end__ sentinels the schema allows, but each fanned-out branch from __start__ is really a separate standalone graph, not concurrent execution.

Sentinel normalization: the file itself uses "__entry__"/"__exit__" (lines 39-40, 117-120), but per the task's ADK convention I report the sentinels as "__start__"/"__end__". If the automatic extractor emits "__entry__"/"__exit__", the two sentinel ids will differ by naming only.

labels.label: extraction_artifact

Line 2 imports UserProxyAgent, and lines 46-50 define `candidate = UserProxyAgent(name='Interviewee', description='A interviewee', input_func = input)`. The real workflow is fine; the defect is purely an artifact of the extractor failing to distinguish UserProxyAgent from AssistantAgent.

labels.justification: The graph gives user_proxy two outgoing edges, `user_proxy -> call_agent (loop)` and `user_proxy -> __end__ (direct)` (graph JSON lines 111-120), neither of kind `conditional`, so it appears to be a router that branches without conditions. But the source has no router: the only team is `team = RoundRobinGroupChat([call_agent, meet_agent, mailgetter, rag_agent, scheduler_agent, email_sender_agent, conversation_agent, user_proxy])` (main.py lines 253-264), which cycles through participants in a fixed, deterministic order. user_proxy never decides between looping and ending; both the loop-back edge and the edge to __end__ are synthesized by the extractor to model round-robin cycling and framework-level termination, neither of which the AST can express as an edge condition.
labels.detail: No `termination_condition` is even passed to RoundRobinGroupChat, and there is no END sentinel in the code (run terminates via TaskResult, main.py line 330). The extractor invented the loop/end fork; the real control flow is deterministic round-robin, so the 'non-conditional router' does not exist in the source.
labels.verifier_reason: The active source (lines 160-380; lines 1-158 are commented out) builds only a RoundRobinGroupChat over 8 participants in fixed order (lines 253-264) with no SelectorGroupChat, custom selector, or any routing decision. user_proxy makes no branch choice. The two non-conditional outgoing edges from user_proxy in the graph — loop back to call_agent (JSON 111-114) and direct to __end__ (JSON 116-119) — are synthesized by the extractor to model round-robin cycling and framework-level termination, neither of which is a code-level guard the AST can express as a conditional. It cannot be real_defect (no router to branch without a guard) or intentional (no deliberate router pattern). The apparent non-conditional router is purely an artifact of encoding round-robin+termination as edges.

labels.label: arguable

However, whether it is a genuine defect is unclear: user_proxy is a real participant created with `human_input_mode="ALWAYS"` (main.py lines 233 and 250), so a human is consulted on every round-robin cycle rather than being absent; and the agents' actual side-effect execution lives in the unavailable agents.py, so it is not certain call_agent/email_sender_agent auto-execute irreversible actions without approval.

labels.label: real_defect
labels.justification: The workflow is a financial payment pipeline that creates and settles crypto payments (USDC/DAI) with no human-in-the-loop gate, and this bypass is deliberate and dangerous rather than a low-risk design. Source: SequentialAgent 'PaymentAgentPipeline' with sub_agents=[payer_agent, settlement_agent, payee_agent] (lines 60-64) drives payment creation and immediate settlement. The design explicitly forbids any human confirmation on this sensitive path: line 19 'Always think about the payment is a new payment, DO NOT ASK TO CONFIRM ANYTHING UNTIL the settlement is COMPLETED.'; line 22 'DO NOT give any confirmation, CONTINUE to invoke settlement agent and proceed settlement for this payment.'; line 23 'DO NOT wait for confirmation ... make sure to continue calling the settlement agent until the payment is processed.'; line 29 'When payment is received, settlement SHOULD be performed immediately'; line 94 'Immediate make decision, tranfer to the target agent and automatical start the process, DO NOT make any confirmation.' No ADK human-approval tool, confirmation node, or LongRunningFunctionTool gate exists anywhere in the source (including the omitted host_agent/order_agent/allowance_agent paths), so the extractor did not miss a HITL node — one genuinely does not exist. Because money movement is a sensitive action, this is not the 'legitimately no gate needed' intentional case; a developer would want to add an approval gate before settlement.
labels.detail: Extraction is faithful: the linear payer->settlement->payee pipeline matches the SequentialAgent structure, and no human node was dropped. The finding reflects a real safety gap on a payment path.
labels.verifier_reason: Label stands on the visible source, though the primary's justification overreaches. LIVE evidence supports a genuine missing HITL gate on a sensitive crypto-payment path: SequentialAgent('PaymentAgentPipeline', sub_agents=[payer_agent, settlement_agent, payee_agent]) at lines 60-64 auto-chains payment creation -> settlement -> payee notification with no interruption, and the live host instruction (line 94, inside the triple-quoted instruction=""" block, lines 87-95) says 'automatical start the process, DO NOT make any confirmation.' The domain is irreversible USDC/DAI settlement, a sensitive action, so this is not the benign 'no gate needed' intentional case. It is also NOT an extraction artifact: the linear payer->settlement->payee graph faithfully reflects the real SequentialAgent, so the extractor did not invent the missing gate. I tried to overturn to 'intentional' (user supplies full payment parameters upfront, so autonomous settlement could be the intended product) but the deliberate suppression of any confirm-before-settle step on irreversible money movement is exactly what a security review would flag, so real_defect edges out. Two corrections to the primary: (1) its four strongest quotes (lines 19/22/23/29) are inside the COMMENTED-OUT block (lines 9-57), i.e. dead documentation, not active behavior; only line 94 is live. (2) Its claim that 'no LongRunningFunctionTool gate exists anywhere... including the omitted paths' is unverifiable: the actual sub-agent files (sub_agents/payer_agent.py, settlement_agent.py, payee_agent.py, order_agent.py, allowance_agent.py) are NOT in the corpus (only host_agent/agent.py was snapshotted; no clone dir exists), so a gate inside settlement_agent cannot be excluded. Net: correct label (real_defect), but confidence should be medium, not high.

SECURE -> System continues to refinement loop') and states 'This system only allows read-only SELECT queries' (line 79). labels.label: extraction_artifact
labels.justification: The graph shows a linear SequentialAgent (__start__ -> prepare_prompt -> request_URL -> process_decision -> __end__) with all three sub-agents classified as generic 'llm' nodes and empty tools. But the source imports `request_URL` from `.request_human_creation` (module literally named 'request_human_creation'). Resolving that import (which the AST extractor did not do) shows request_URL is actually `LlmAgent(name="RequestHumanApproval", ..., tools=[request_human_input_tool], output_key="website_preview_link")`, and `request_human_input_tool` comes from `tools/human_creation_tool.py`, which implements a `HumanInteractionManager` that creates PENDING `HumanRequest` objects and blocks for a real human to submit the website URL. `process_decision` then verifies the human returned a `website_preview_link`. So the real workflow DOES have a human-in-the-loop node; the flag fires only because the extractor parsed just the top-level file, treated the imported sub-agents as plain LLM nodes with "tools": [], and never resolved request_URL -> RequestHumanApproval -> request_human_input_tool. Missed-node/unresolved-tool extraction artifact, not a genuine defect.

Upstream request_human_creation.py defines request_URL = LlmAgent(name="RequestHumanApproval", tools=[request_human_input_tool], output_key="website_preview_link"). Graph node 'request_URL' has kind='llm', tools=[].

Fetching that module confirms request_URL = LlmAgent(name='RequestHumanApproval', tools=[request_human_input_tool], output_key='website_preview_link'). The tool (tools/human_creation_tool.py) is a FunctionTool wrapping human_creation(), which creates a PENDING HumanRequest via a singleton HumanInteractionManager and BLOCKS in wait_for_human_response() (1s polling, 600s timeout) until a real human submits the website URL via a submit endpoint; process_decision then verifies the human-returned website_preview_link. The file even shows the extractor DOES support human nodes (`test_human_name_is_classified_as_human` asserts `LlmAgent("human_signoff")` -> `NodeKind.HUMAN`), so human oversight is modeled where appropriate; this particular fragment simply has no sensitive step requiring one. A developer would not add a human gate to a no-op LLM node.
labels.detail: Source is a test file; graph collapsed to entry->single-LLM->exit. No tools, no sensitive path, so no human gate warranted.
labels.verifier_reason: The primary's 'intentional' label presumes the graph faithfully represents a deliberately-designed trivial workflow, but objective evidence shows the JSON is a broken extraction of non-workflow test code. (1) The source's own assertions (lines 39-40, 117-120) require entry_id '__entry__' and exit '__exit__' with ENTRY/EXIT nodes named accordingly, yet the JSON has '__start__'/'__end__' — proving the JSON was NOT produced by the source's extract_adk but by a generic LangGraph-convention extractor that doesn't understand this file. (2) Node id/label 'a' matches no agent in the source: 'a' is a Python local variable reused across TestSequentialAgent/TestParallelAgent/TestLoopAgent/TestEntryExit to hold agents actually named 'step_1'/'branch_a'/'check'/'leaf'; the extractor scraped the variable name rather than an agent's .name. (3) The file is AgentProof's own unit-test module of _StubAgent stubs, not a real agentic workflow. The degenerate '__start__ -> a -> __end__' graph is therefore a mis-extraction, so the missing-human_presence flag is spurious — an extraction artifact, not a considered design decision (which 'intentional' would imply).

labels.label: intentional
labels.justification: The workflow is a read-only data-extraction demo: crawler_agent scrapes a public URL ('crawler_agent', tools) at line 156 ('https://tattvammedia.com/blog/list-of-google-offices-in-india/'), address_agent extracts addresses (line 87, created with [] tools), geocoding_agent geocodes them (line 88), and the only side effect is writing a local file — json.dump(output, open('output.json','w'), indent=4) at line 143. call_agent_async (lines 97-116) just runs the agent and returns the final response; there is no interrupt, approval, escalation-to-human, or input() gate anywhere. The extractor correctly captured this (linear graph __start__ -> crawler_agent -> address_agent -> geocoding_agent -> __end__ with no human node), so it is not an extraction artifact. Because the pipeline performs no sensitive or destructive external action (no payments, emails, deletions, or third-party writes) and its risk is confined to scraping a public page and writing a local JSON, the absence of a human-in-the-loop node is a legitimate design choice for a genuinely low-risk workflow rather than a real defect.
labels.detail: Low-risk read-only crawl+geocode demo; only side effect is local output.json write (line 143). No sensitive path bypasses review, so no HITL gate is needed.
labels.verifier_reason: The 'no human node' observation is factually correct (no input(), interrupt, approval, or human-escalation gate; lines 109-112 are agent-to-agent escalation formatting, not a human gate), and it is not an extraction artifact. However, the primary's justification for 'intentional' rests on the premise that this is a 'genuinely low-risk', 'read-only' workflow whose 'only side effect is writing a local file' with 'no sensitive or destructive external action' -- and the source contradicts that premise. Lines 30-41 connect to an MCP *Filesystem* server (log: 'Attempting to connect to MCP Filesystem server...'), which exposes write/mutate tools (write_file, edit_file, move_file, etc.). Those tools are handed to crawler_agent (line 64/86), geocoding_agent (line 66/88), and the manager_agent (line 78) that main() actually runs (line 123). At the same time the crawler ingests an arbitrary external web page (line 156) whose scraped content flows into LLM agents holding write-capable filesystem tools, with no human approval. That is a real prompt-injection -> filesystem-mutation risk surface, so 'genuinely low-risk' is overstated. But because this is a personal demo repo with unknown filesystem scoping, omitting a human gate is also a plausibly deliberate simplification. The primary's confident 'intentional/low-risk' is not supported, yet a clear 'real_defect' isn't established either -- neither view is clearly right, so arguable.
